**Auckland Castle**, also known as **Auckland Palace**, is a former bishop's palace located in the town of [Bishop Auckland](/wiki/Bishop_Auckland "Bishop Auckland") in [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham"), England. The castle was a residence of the [bishops of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham") from approximately 1183 and was their primary residence between 1832 and 2012, when the castle and its contents were sold to the Auckland Castle Trust (now the Auckland Project).[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is now a tourist attraction, but still houses the bishop's offices.

The castle is notable for its chapel, described as "one of the finest rooms in North East England" in the *[Buildings of England](/wiki/Buildings_of_England "Buildings of England")* series,[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) which was the medieval great hall until it was remodelled by Bishop [John Cosin](/wiki/John_Cosin "John Cosin") in 1661–1665. The woodwork, which includes the pulpit, stalls, and screen, was commissioned by Cosin and combines [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") and [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture") forms.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) The castle also contains twelve paintings depicting *[Jacob and His Twelve Sons](/wiki/Jacob_and_His_Twelve_Sons "Jacob and His Twelve Sons")* by the Spanish painter [Francisco de Zurbarán](/wiki/Francisco_de_Zurbar%C3%A1n "Francisco de Zurbarán"); the thirteenth portrait, *[Benjamin](/wiki/Benjamin "Benjamin")*, is a copy, as the original hangs in [Grimsthorpe Castle](/wiki/Grimsthorpe_Castle "Grimsthorpe Castle"), Lincolnshire. Auckland Castle is a [grade I listed](/wiki/Grade_I_listed "Grade I listed") building.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland_Castle&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Auckland Castle occupies an area of flat ground between the [River Gaunless](/wiki/River_Gaunless "River Gaunless") and [River Wear](/wiki/River_Wear "River Wear"), south of their confluence. The town of Bishop Auckland abuts the castle to the west, and on the other three sides the land falls away steeply to the rivers.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) The castle was probably begun by [Hugh de Puiset](/wiki/Hugh_de_Puiset "Hugh de Puiset"), who was bishop from 1153 to 1195, and completed in the first half of the thirteenth century.[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4) The present chapel survives from his building, where it served as the great hall.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

A college for a dean and nine [canons](/wiki/Canon_regular "Canon regular") was established immediately west of the castle's enclosing wall by [Bishop Booth](/wiki/Lawrence_Booth "Lawrence Booth") in the fifteenth century.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

In 1603 after the [Union of the Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns"), [Tobias Matthew](/wiki/Tobias_Matthew "Tobias Matthew") invited [Anne of Denmark](/wiki/Anne_of_Denmark "Anne of Denmark"), [Prince Henry](/wiki/Henry_Frederick,_Prince_of_Wales "Henry Frederick, Prince of Wales"), and [Princess Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_Stuart,_Queen_of_Bohemia "Elizabeth Stuart, Queen of Bohemia") to stay at Auckland on their journey from Scotland to London.[[5]](#cite_note-5) After the disestablishment of the Church of England at the end of the [First English Civil War](/wiki/First_English_Civil_War "First English Civil War") in 1646, Auckland Castle was sold to Sir [Arthur Haselrig](/wiki/Arthur_Haselrig "Arthur Haselrig"), who demolished much of the medieval building, including the original two-storey chapel, and built a [mansion](/wiki/Mansion "Mansion").[[6]](#cite_note-Lightfoot_1892_140-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) After the [Restoration](/wiki/English_Restoration "English Restoration") of the Monarchy, [Bishop](/wiki/Prince-Bishop "Prince-Bishop") [John Cosin](/wiki/John_Cosin "John Cosin") in turn demolished Hazelrigg's mansion and rebuilt the castle, converting the banqueting hall into the [chapel](/wiki/Chapel "Chapel") that stands today.[[6]](#cite_note-Lightfoot_1892_140-6)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In 1756, [Bishop Richard Trevor](/wiki/Richard_Trevor_(bishop) "Richard Trevor (bishop)") bought the notable set of paintings, [Jacob and his twelve sons](/wiki/Jacob_and_his_twelve_sons "Jacob and his twelve sons"), by [Francisco de Zurbarán](/wiki/Francisco_de_Zurbar%C3%A1n "Francisco de Zurbarán") which still hang in the Long Dining Room. It is possible that the seventeenth century paintings were intended for South America. However they never reached their supposed destination, eventually coming into the possession of James Mendez who sold twelve of the thirteen to [Bishop Trevor](/wiki/Richard_Trevor_(bishop) "Richard Trevor (bishop)") in 1757.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Bishop Trevor was unable to secure the 13th portrait, [Benjamin](/wiki/Benjamin "Benjamin"), which was sold separately to the [Duke of Ancaster](/wiki/Duke_of_Ancaster "Duke of Ancaster") and hangs in [Grimsthorpe Castle](/wiki/Grimsthorpe_Castle "Grimsthorpe Castle"), [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire "Lincolnshire"). Bishop Trevor commissioned [Arthur Pond](/wiki/Arthur_Pond "Arthur Pond") to produce a copy painting of "Benjamin". The copy, together with the 12 originals, hang in the castle's Long Dining Room, which Bishop Trevor had redesigned especially to take the pictures.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/The_Throne_Room%2C_Auckland_Castle.jpg/250px-The_Throne_Room%2C_Auckland_Castle.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Throne_Room,_Auckland_Castle.jpg)

The Throne Room

[Shute Barrington](/wiki/Shute_Barrington "Shute Barrington"), Bishop of Durham from 1791 to 1826, employed the eminent architect [James Wyatt](/wiki/James_Wyatt "James Wyatt") to match the disparate architecture of the palace in the late 18th century, including its Throne Room and Garden Screen. In 1832, when [William van Mildert](/wiki/William_van_Mildert "William van Mildert"), the last bishop to rule the [county palatine of Durham](/wiki/County_Palatine_of_Durham "County Palatine of Durham"), gave over [Durham Castle](/wiki/Durham_Castle "Durham Castle") to found [Durham University](/wiki/Durham_University "Durham University"), Auckland Castle became the sole episcopal seat of the [See of Durham](/wiki/See_of_Durham "See of Durham").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In 2001 the [Church Commissioners](/wiki/Church_Commissioners "Church Commissioners") voted to sell the paintings, a decision that was revoked in 2011 following a donation of £15 million by investment manager and philanthropist [Jonathan Ruffer](/wiki/Jonathan_Ruffer "Jonathan Ruffer"); new arrangements placed the paintings, along with the castle, under the Auckland Castle Trust, making them available to the public after centuries during which they hung in a private home where they could be seen only by invited guests or by special arrangement with the Bishop's staff.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### 2019 re-opening

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland_Castle&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 2019 re-opening")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/The_Dining_Room_at_Auckland_Castle.jpg/500px-The_Dining_Room_at_Auckland_Castle.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Dining_Room_at_Auckland_Castle.jpg)

The dining room with the paintings of Jacob and his Twelve Sons by [Francisco de Zurbarán](/wiki/Francisco_de_Zurbar%C3%A1n "Francisco de Zurbarán")

News reports in 2019 clarified the situation, stating that in 2012, Ruffer had purchased the castle and all of the contents, including the artwork, which included the works by Francisco de Zurbarán.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The paintings, which had been on tour, were returned to the site in time for the re-opening of the castle to visitors on 2 November 2019 as the Auckland Project,[[18]](#cite_note-18) after a multi-million pound restoration project, funded partly by the National Lottery.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

By the time of the opening day, a new 115-foot (35 m) high tower had been erected as a visitor centre; the structure has a lift and a staircase as well as balconies for views of the castle from above. The interior had been fully restored, including the bishops' "palatial" quarters. According to one news item, "each of the 14 restored rooms, recreated from contemporary accounts and personal recollections" features the career of one former bishop.[[22]](#cite_note-theartnewspaper.com-22) The Faith Museum of world religion and a huge glass greenhouse were under construction on Castle property.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Other attractions already operating at or near the Castle include the Mining Art Gallery (in a nearby former bank building)[[22]](#cite_note-theartnewspaper.com-22) showing work mainly by self-taught or night school-educated miners; this attraction opened in 2017 (thanks to support provided to the Castle Trust by Bishop Auckland and Shildon AAP and Durham County Council);[[24]](#cite_note-24) an open-air theatre, [Kynren](/wiki/Kynren "Kynren"), depicting "An Epic Tale of England" with a cast of 1,000; and the Bishop Trevor Gallery at the Castle; the latter started displaying the [National Gallery](/wiki/National_Gallery "National Gallery")'s Masterpiece touring exhibit in October 2019.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In October 2023, the Faith Museum opened to the public. Designed by [Niall McLaughlin Architects](/wiki/Niall_McLaughlin_Architects "Niall McLaughlin Architects"), it covers 6000 years of British religious history, from the [Neolithic period](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") to 2000AD.[[26]](#cite_note-26) It is housed in the Scotland Wing of the castle, as well as a new stone-built extension.[[27]](#cite_note-gate-27)

In May 2024, the 17th century walled gardens reopened, with a new glasshouse and faith garden.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The Great Garden is set to open in 2025.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Bek's chapel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland_Castle&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Bek's chapel")]

In February 2020, it was announced that the foundations of Bek's chapel had been found at the castle by archeologists. It had long been known that the chapel, which would have been one of the largest in Europe, was located on the castle grounds, but its location was unknown. The chapel was built by the warrior-bishop [Antony Bek](/wiki/Antony_Bek_(bishop_of_Durham) "Antony Bek (bishop of Durham)") in roughly 1300 at a cost of £148.[[30]](#cite_note-30) There was a special exhibition at Auckland Castle from 4 March 2020 to 6 September 2020 to display items found in the excavations.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Archaeology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland_Castle&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Archaeology")]

In June 2024, archaeologists working on the Auckland Project announced the discovery of a Golden Primrose flower decoration along with at least seven large wall remains and cellars filled with rubble. The wall remains are believed to be part of a [rectangular](/wiki/Rectangle "Rectangle") building that belonged to [Arthur Haselrig](/wiki/Arthur_Haselrig "Arthur Haselrig").[[32]](#cite_note-32)

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland_Castle&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Description")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/The_Gate_at_Auckland_Castle.jpg/250px-The_Gate_at_Auckland_Castle.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Gate_at_Auckland_Castle.jpg)

The clock tower

The castle grounds are entered from Bishop Auckland marketplace through the clock tower, which was built in 1760 by [Bishop Trevor](/wiki/Richard_Trevor_(bishop) "Richard Trevor (bishop)") in the [Gothick](/wiki/Gothick_architecture "Gothick architecture") style.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[33]](#cite_note-33) The range which flanks it to the right incorporates the remains of the south range of the fifteenth century college mentioned above; the north and east ranges behind also survive in part.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) Within the gates, the Faith Museum is to the north, within a gabled stone building intended to reflect the form of medieval tithe barns. To the south are the walled gardens and glasshouse, below a terrace. A castellated screen wall built for Bishop [Shute Barrington](/wiki/Shute_Barrington "Shute Barrington") in 1795 separates the terrace from the castle proper.[[34]](#cite_note-ScreenWall-34)

The main buildings form two sides of a courtyard. To the north is the large four-bay aisled chapel, originally the hall of [Bishop Pudsey](/wiki/Hugh_de_Puiset "Hugh de Puiset")'s palace. This was built in *c.*1190 with unusually ornate arcades and capitals, and remodelled in *c.*1300 for [Bishop Bek](/wiki/Antony_Bek_(bishop_of_Durham) "Antony Bek (bishop of Durham)"). In 1661-65 it was converted for use as a chapel by [Bishop Cosin](/wiki/John_Cosin "John Cosin"), the original chapel having been destroyed in the Interregnum. At this time the exterior was heavily remodelled in the mixture of Gothic and Baroque characteristic of Cosin, and the clerestory was replaced.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) The western range housed the bishop's state rooms above the offices and service functions (now the café). At its heart this structure is that of Bek's extensive reconstruction, but this is now concealed by work of the 16th and 18th centuries. The state rooms as they now appear are largely the work of [Wyatt](/wiki/James_Wyatt "James Wyatt") in *c.*1795 for Bishop Barrington. They are in the Gothick style and include along with the normal suite of rooms an ante-room and throne room, as the Prince-Bishop of Durham had until the 19th century quasi-regal powers.[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4) The Long Dining Room houses the Zurbarán paintings, while the other rooms contain exhibits on the history of the castle, and the Bishop Trevor art gallery. Behind this range is the Scotland Wing, originally [Bishop Tunstall](/wiki/Cuthbert_Tunstall "Cuthbert Tunstall")'s long gallery, but now part of the Faith Museum.[[27]](#cite_note-gate-27)

The Castle is surrounded by a deer park of 800 acres (320 ha) of [parkland](/wiki/Landscaped_parkland "Landscaped parkland"). It retains many of the medieval elements, including the fish ponds and woodland paths, providing an important record of how the medieval bishops lived, entertained and hunted there.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland_Castle&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Culture")]

The castle was used as the backdrop for [Lewis Carroll](/wiki/Lewis_Carroll "Lewis Carroll")'s story "A Legend of Scotland" in the 19th century.[[36]](#cite_note-scotsman-36) In 2006, Auckland Castle was the setting for two episodes of [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC")'s *[Antiques Roadshow](/wiki/Antiques_Roadshow "Antiques Roadshow")*.[[37]](#cite_note-37) In 2013, a 15th-century bed once owned by [Henry VII](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England") was put on display at the castle.[[38]](#cite_note-38)
